perturbation.original_text: The term "Confidential Information" means any information or material which is proprietary to the Owner, whether or not owned or developed by the Owner, which is not generally known other than by the Owner, and which the Recipient may obtain through any direct or indirect contact with the Owner.
perturbation.changed_text: The term "Confidential Information" means any information or material which the Owner deems confidential, regardless of whether it is generally known or independently developed by the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: This change broadens the definition of "Confidential Information" to include information that might be publicly known or independently developed, conflicting with standard legal definitions and potentially hindering fair use or prior knowledge defenses.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the UTSA's definition of a trade secret, which requires that the information not be generally known or readily ascertainable. The modified definition attempts to classify any information the Owner deems confidential as such, regardless of its public availability.

perturbation.justification: The change in the contract broadens the definition of "Confidential Information" to include information that might be publicly known, which contradicts the general understanding and legal definitions, as per the Perturbation Explanation. The Law Explanation indicates this contradicts the UTSA's definition of a trade secret, which requires information not to be generally known. This misalignment with established legal standards for confidential information creates a legal contradiction as described under the Misaligned Terminology definition, potentially rendering the clause unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: During the term of this Agreement and for 3 Years thereafter, Recipient will not attempt to do business with, or otherwise solicit any business contacts found or otherwise referred by Owner to Recipient for the purpose of circumventing, the result of whic h shall be to prevent the Owner from realizing or recognizing a profit, fees, or otherwise, without the specific written approval of the Owner.
perturbation.changed_text: During the term of this Agreement and perpetually thereafter, Recipient will not attempt to do business with any contacts associated with the Owner, regardless of the source of the contact or the purpose of the business.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-circumvention clause perpetually is likely unenforceable and contradicts reasonable limitations on non-compete agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 542.335 sets limitations on the duration of non-compete agreements. A perpetual restriction is likely to be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under Florida law.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIII > Chapter 542 > Section 335 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XXXIII REGULATION OF TRADE, COMMERCE, INVESTMENTS, AND SOLICITATIONS Chapter 542 COMBINATIONS RESTRICTING TRADE OR COMMERCE Entire Chapter SECTION 335 Valid restraints of trade or commerce. F.S. 542.335 542.335 Valid restraints of trade or commerce. — (1) Notwithstanding s. 542.18 and subsection (2), enforcement of contracts that restrict or prohibit competition during or after the term of restrictive covenants, so long as such contracts are reasonable in time, area, and line of business, is not prohibited. In any action concerning enforcement of a restrictive covenant: (a) A court shall not enforce a restrictive covenant unless it is set forth in a writing signed by the person against whom enforcement is sought. (b) The person seeking enforcement of a restrictive covenant shall plead and prove the existence of one or more legitimate business interests justifying the restrictive covenant. The term “legitimate business interest” includes, but is not limited to: 1. Trade secrets, as defined in s. 688.002 (4). 2. Valuable confidential business or professional information that otherwise does not qualify as trade secrets. 3. Substantial relationships with specific prospective or existing customers, patients, or clients. 4. Customer, patient, or client goodwill associated with: a. An ongoing business or professional practice, by way of trade name, trademark, service mark, or “trade dress”; b. A specific geographic location; or c. A specific marketing or trade area. 5. Extraordinary or specialized training. Any restrictive covenant not supported by a legitimate business interest is unlawful and is void and unenforceable. (c) A person seeking enforcement of a restrictive covenant also shall plead and prove that the contractually specified restraint is reasonably necessary to protect the legitimate business interest or interests justifying the restriction. If a person seeking enforcement of the restrictive covenant establishes prima facie that the restraint is reasonably necessary, the person opposing enforcement has the burden of establishing that the contractually specified restraint is overbroad, overlong, or otherwise not reasonably necessary to protect the established
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-circumvention clause to be perpetual, which contradicts the principle that non-compete agreements must be reasonable in time. Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing Florida Statute 542.335, states that contracts restricting competition are enforceable 'so long as such contracts are reasonable in time, area, and line of business'. The 'perpetually thereafter' timeframe in the changed text is highly likely to be deemed unreasonable under this statute, thereby creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of confidentiality shall survive indefinitely from the date of disclosure of the Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of confidentiality shall terminate one year from the date of the agreement, regardless of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Shortening the confidentiality period to one year, regardless of disclosure, may violate reasonable expectations and trade secret protections.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA protects trade secrets as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. A one-year limit on confidentiality, regardless of whether the information has become public, undermines this protection.

perturbation.justification: The original text ensures indefinite confidentiality, while the changed text limits it to one year. The Law Explanation, generated by the LLM, indicates that the UTSA (Uniform Trade Secrets Act) protects trade secrets as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. By limiting confidentiality to one year, the changed text directly undermines the protection offered by the UTSA, regardless of whether the information remains a trade secret. This creates a contradiction by potentially allowing the trade secret to be disclosed after one year, thus violating the protections intended by the UTSA. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a 'Page Not Found' error related to the Uniform Law Commission website, the Law Explanation is sufficiently indicative of a conflict to justify the existence of a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In any legal action between the parties concerning this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover reasonable attorneys fees and costs.
perturbation.changed_text: In any legal action between the parties concerning this Agreement, each party shall bear its own attorneys' fees and costs, regardless of the outcome.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the provision to deny attorneys' fees to the prevailing party contradicts the general principle of fee-shifting in contract disputes, which can be permitted or required by statute or case law.
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can agree to bear their own fees, Florida Statute 57.105 allows for attorney's fees to be awarded against a party who raises a frivolous claim or defense. The modified text prevents this even in cases of frivolous litigation.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title VI > Chapter 57 > Section 105 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title VI CIVIL PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE Chapter 57 COURT COSTS Entire Chapter SECTION 105 Attorney’s fee; sanctions for raising unsupported claims or defenses; exceptions; service of motions; damages for delay of litigation. F.S. 57.105 57.105 Attorney’s fee; sanctions for raising unsupported claims or defenses; exceptions; service of motions; damages for delay of litigation. — (1) Upon the court’s initiative or motion of any party, the court shall award a reasonable attorney’s fee, including prejudgment interest, to be paid to the prevailing party in equal amounts by the losing party and the losing party’s attorney on any claim or defense at any time during a civil proceeding or action in which the court finds that the losing party or the losing party’s attorney knew or should have known that a claim or defense when initially presented to the court or at any time before trial: (a) Was not supported by the material facts necessary to establish the claim or defense; or (b) Would not be supported by the application of then-existing law to those material facts. (2) At any time in any civil proceeding or action in which the moving party proves by a preponderance of the evidence that any action taken by the opposing party, including, but not limited to, the filing of any pleading or part thereof, the assertion of or response to any discovery demand, the assertion of any claim or defense, or the response to any request by any other party, was taken primarily for the purpose of unreasonable delay, the court shall award damages to the moving party for its reasonable expenses incurred in obtaining the order, which may include attorney’s fees, and other loss resulting from the improper delay. (3) Notwithstanding subsections (1) and (2), monetary sanctions may not be awarded: (a) Under paragraph (1)(b) if the court determines that the claim or defense was initially presented to the court as a good faith argument for the extension, modification, or reversal of existing law or the establishment of new law, as it applied to the material facts, with a reasonable expectation of success. (b) Under paragraph (1)(a) or paragraph (1)(b) against the losing party’s attorney if he or she has acted in good faith, based o
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the possibility of awarding attorney's fees to the prevailing party, even in cases where the losing party's claim or defense was frivolous. Florida Statute 57.105(1) explicitly states that a court 'shall award a reasonable attorney’s fee' to the prevailing party if the losing party knew or should have known that their claim or defense was not supported by facts or law. The modified text, by stating each party bears their own fees 'regardless of the outcome', eliminates the court's ability to impose sanctions as detailed in the statute, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed under the laws of the State of Florida.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be construed under the laws of the People's Republic of China, excluding its conflict of law principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to that of a foreign jurisdiction with potentially different legal standards can undermine the enforceability and predictability of the contract, particularly if the parties and subject matter are primarily located in Florida.
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can generally choose the governing law, courts may refuse to enforce a choice-of-law provision if the chosen state has no substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction and there is no reasonable basis for the parties' choice. This section of the Restatement highlights this as a limit to contractual freedom.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from Florida to the People's Republic of China, as explained in the Perturbation Explanation, introduces a potential contradiction. The Law Explanation notes that courts may refuse to enforce a choice-of-law provision if the chosen state has no substantial relationship to the parties or transaction and there is no reasonable basis for the parties' choice. Snippet 2 defines conflict of laws as a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions and states that the court must determine what law to apply in accordance with the law of the forum. Therefore, changing the governing law creates a contradiction because it could be deemed unenforceable if there's no reasonable basis for choosing Chinese law, potentially requiring the court to apply the law of the forum (Florida), creating uncertainty about the contract's enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees to defend, indemnify, and hold harmless the other party and its officers, directors, agents, affiliates, distributors, representatives, and employees from any and all third party claims, demands, liabilities, costs and expenses, including reasonable attorneys fees, costs and expenses resulting from the indemnifying party's material breach of any duty, representation, or warranty under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees to indemnify the other party for any loss or damage, irrespective of whether the loss or damage arises from the other party’s gross negligence or willful misconduct.
perturbation.explanation: Indemnifying a party for its own gross negligence or willful misconduct is against public policy in many jurisdictions, including Florida, and potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida law and public policy regarding exculpatory clauses.
perturbation.law_citation: Dyson v. Stainback, 16 Fla. L. Weekly Supp. 789a (Fla. 15th Cir. Ct. 2009)
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida law generally disfavors exculpatory clauses that relieve a party from liability for its own gross negligence or intentional misconduct. While this isn't a direct statute, cases demonstrate that this kind of exculpatory arrangement goes against public policy.

perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates indemnification even for the indemnitee's gross negligence or willful misconduct. The Law Explanation states that Florida law disfavors exculpatory clauses relieving a party from liability for its own gross negligence or intentional misconduct, suggesting a public policy against such arrangements. While Snippet 1 is missing, and Snippet 2 only generally speaks about access to law, the Law Explanation, combined with the Perturbation Explanation, indicates that enforcing indemnification for one's own gross negligence contradicts established legal principles in Florida, thereby creating a legal contradiction.
